walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Treasure Cards
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Dominion set up. First, find the three types of Treasure cards. Copper cards have a brown border and are worth one coin. Silver cards have a gray border and are worth two coins. Gold cards have a yellow border and are worth three coins. Make three separate piles and place them in a row in the center of the table where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Victory Cards
walkthrough.text: Next, find the three types of Victory cards. These have green borders. Estates are worth one victory point, Duchies are worth three victory points, and Provinces are worth six victory points. Make three separate face-up piles next to the Treasure piles. For a two-player game, use eight of each. For three or four players, use twelve of each.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Curse Pile
walkthrough.text: Now find the Curse cards. These have a dark purple border and are worth negative one victory point. Nobody wants these, but some Kingdom cards force them on your opponents. Make one face-up pile. For two players, use ten Curses. For three players, use twenty. For four players, use thirty.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Trash Pile Area
walkthrough.text: Designate a spot on the table for the Trash pile. This is where cards go when they are permanently removed from the game. The Trash is not anyone's discard pile. Once a card is trashed, it is gone for good. You can place a placeholder card or just leave an empty space.

walkthrough.title: Choose Ten Kingdom Card Piles
walkthrough.text: Here is where Dominion gets exciting. The box comes with twenty-five different types of Kingdom cards, but you only use ten types per game. For your first game, the rulebook suggests a specific set of ten. You can also pick randomly or choose your favorites. Each Kingdom pile gets ten copies stacked face up. These ten piles, along with the Treasures, Victory cards, and Curses, form the Supply.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Supply
walkthrough.text: Line up all ten Kingdom card piles in a row or grid where everyone can read them. It helps to sort them by cost from low to high, left to right. Place them near the Treasure and Victory piles so the entire Supply is visible. Every player should be able to see every pile and how many cards remain in each.

walkthrough.title: Read the Kingdom Cards Aloud
walkthrough.text: Before dealing starting decks, take a moment to read each of the ten Kingdom cards out loud. Hold up each one so everyone can see the art and text. This is important because the Kingdom cards change every game. Make sure everyone understands what each card does before you start playing.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Decks
walkthrough.text: Now let's build each player's starting deck. Give every player seven Copper cards and three Estate cards. That is ten cards total. These are taken from the Copper pile and Estate pile in the Supply. Everyone starts with the exact same deck, so the game is perfectly fair from the beginning.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Draw Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player shuffles their ten-card deck thoroughly and places it face down in front of them. This is your personal draw pile. Now draw the top five cards from your draw pile into your hand. Keep your hand hidden from other players. This is your starting hand for the very first turn.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Play Area
walkthrough.text: Each player needs space for four things in front of them. Your draw pile goes face down on one side. Your discard pile will go face up next to it. Leave space in the middle for cards you play during your turn. And keep your hand of cards hidden. Make sure everyone has enough room. You will be surprised how many cards pile up as the game goes on.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first by any method you like. Play will go clockwise from there. The first player does have a slight advantage, but it evens out quickly. Once everyone has their five-card hand drawn and the Supply is laid out, you are ready to start playing. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Every turn in Dominion follows the same three phases in order. First is the Action Phase, where you can play Action cards from your hand. Second is the Buy Phase, where you play Treasure cards and buy new cards from the Supply. Third is the Clean-up Phase, where you discard everything and draw a fresh hand. Let's walk through each phase in detail.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase: The Basics
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, you get one Action to spend. You can play one Action card from your hand by placing it face up in your play area and doing what it says. If you do not have any Action cards in your hand, or you do not want to play one, you simply skip this phase and move on to buying. Many beginners forget that you only get one Action to start with, which is important to keep in mind.

walkthrough.title: Action Cards and Their Effects
walkthrough.text: Action cards can do many things, and they will tell you exactly what to do in their text box. The most common effects are plus Cards, which lets you draw more cards from your deck. Plus Actions, which gives you additional Actions to play more Action cards this turn. Plus Coins, which adds virtual coins to spend during your Buy Phase. And plus Buy, which lets you purchase more than one card this turn. Follow the instructions on the card from top to bottom.

walkthrough.title: Chaining Actions Together
walkthrough.text: Here is where it gets fun. If an Action card gives you plus Actions, you can play another Action card after it. For example, if you play a Village which gives you plus two Actions and plus one Card, you now have two Actions remaining. You could play two more Action cards this turn. If one of those also gives plus Actions, the chain continues. This is the heart of Dominion strategy, building chains of Actions that let you play many cards in a single turn.

walkthrough.title: Terminal Actions
walkthrough.text: An Action card that does not give you plus Actions is called a terminal action. Once you play a terminal, your Action Phase is over unless you already have extra Actions banked from earlier cards. This is a critical concept. If you load your deck with too many terminal actions and no cards that give plus Actions, you will constantly draw hands with two terminals and only be able to play one. We will talk about this more in the practice tutorial.

walkthrough.title: Buy Phase: Playing Treasures
walkthrough.text: After your Action Phase ends, it is time to buy. First, play all the Treasure cards from your hand face up into your play area. Copper is worth one coin, Silver is worth two, and Gold is worth three. Add up the total coins from your Treasures plus any coins you earned from Action cards during the Action Phase. This total is how much you can spend this turn.

walkthrough.title: Buy Phase: Buying a Card
walkthrough.text: To buy a card, choose any card from the Supply whose cost is equal to or less than your total coins. Take one copy from the top of that pile and place it face up on your discard pile. Not your hand, not your deck, your discard pile. You do not get to use a newly purchased card until it cycles through your deck later. If you cannot afford anything or do not want to buy, you can choose to buy nothing.

walkthrough.title: Buy Phase: Multiple Buys
walkthrough.text: If you have extra Buys from Action cards, you can purchase multiple cards in one turn. Each Buy is a separate purchase. You spend coins from your total pool, but coins do not carry over between Buys automatically. For example, if you have seven coins and two Buys, you could buy a card costing four and then another costing three. You could not buy two cards costing five each because you only have seven coins total.

walkthrough.title: Clean-up Phase
walkthrough.text: This part is simple but very important. Take all the cards in your play area, everything you played this turn, both Action cards and Treasures. Also take any cards remaining in your hand that you did not play. Put all of them together into your discard pile, face up. Then draw five new cards from your draw pile. That is your hand for your next turn. Your turn is now over and play passes to the next player clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Reshuffling Your Deck
walkthrough.text: Eventually your draw pile will run out of cards. When you need to draw and your draw pile is empty, shuffle your entire discard pile to form a new draw pile, then continue drawing. This is a fundamental part of Dominion. Every card you buy will eventually show up in your hand because your deck recycles. This means that junk cards like Coppers and Estates keep coming back unless you find a way to trash them.

walkthrough.title: Gaining vs. Buying
walkthrough.text: There is an important difference between gaining and buying. When you buy a card, you spend coins during your Buy Phase and use up one of your Buys. When you gain a card, you get it for free through a card effect, without spending coins or using a Buy. In both cases, the card goes to your discard pile unless the card effect says otherwise. Some powerful Action cards let you gain cards directly, which is like getting a free purchase.

walkthrough.title: Trashing Cards
walkthrough.text: Some Kingdom cards let you trash cards from your hand or from play. When you trash a card, it goes to the Trash pile in the center of the table and is permanently removed from your deck. This is extremely powerful. Your starting Coppers and Estates are weak cards that clog your deck. Trashing them means your remaining cards cycle faster and your hands become more consistent. If the ten Kingdom cards include a trasher, pay close attention to it.

walkthrough.title: Attack Cards and Reactions
walkthrough.text: Some Kingdom cards are Attack cards, shown by the word Attack in their type line. When you play an Attack, it affects all other players in some way, like forcing them to discard cards or gain Curses. Reaction cards can be revealed from your hand in response to an Attack to protect yourself. The most common Reaction is Moat, which blocks all effects of an Attack when revealed. If someone plays an Attack, always check if you have a Reaction card in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Game End Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when one of two things happens. Either the Province pile runs out completely, or any three Supply piles are empty. This includes any piles at all, Kingdom cards, Treasures, Curses, or Victory cards. When the game end is triggered, the current player finishes their turn and then the game is over. Everyone counts their victory points.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Gather all of your cards from your draw pile, discard pile, and hand. Count up all the victory points from your Victory cards. Estates are worth one, Duchies are worth three, and Provinces are worth six. Subtract one point for each Curse card you have. Some Kingdom cards also have victory point values printed on them. Add those in too. The player with the most total victory points wins. If there is a tie, the tied player who took fewer turns wins. If still tied, you share the victory.

bullets: Each turn has 3 phases: Action → Buy → Clean-up

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through a few scripted turns with two example players so you can see how everything works. Player 1 and Player 2 each have their starting deck of seven Coppers and three Estates, shuffled and with five cards drawn. The Supply is set up with ten Kingdom piles. Ready? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws their first hand: four Coppers and one Estate. Since there are no Action cards in the starting deck, the Action Phase is skipped entirely. That is perfectly normal for the first couple of turns. Nobody has any Actions yet because nobody has bought any Kingdom cards.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Buys Silver
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays all four Coppers for a total of four coins. They look at the Supply and decide to buy a Silver, which costs three coins. The Silver goes onto Player 1's discard pile. They have one coin left over, but unused coins are lost at the end of the turn. Now they put everything, the played Coppers, the unplayed Estate, and the new Silver, all into the discard pile. Draw five new cards. Done.

walkthrough.title: Why Silver Is a Good Early Buy
walkthrough.text: Buying Silver early is one of the safest plays in Dominion. It adds two coins to future hands, making it easier to afford more expensive cards later. Compare that to buying a card costing two or three, which might be useful but does not increase your buying power. Silver is boring but reliable, and most strong openings include at least one Silver.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Buying an Action Card
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws three Coppers and two Estates. That is only three coins. They look at the Kingdom cards and spot a Village that costs three coins. Village gives plus one Card and plus two Actions. Player 2 buys the Village. It goes to their discard pile. Good choice. Next turn they still will not have the Village in hand, but once their deck reshuffles, it will show up and start powering their Action chains.

walkthrough.title: The Deck Cycling Concept
walkthrough.text: Here is something crucial to understand. When you buy a card, it goes to your discard pile, not your hand. You will not see that card until you draw through your entire draw pile, reshuffle your discard, and draw it into a future hand. In the early game with only ten cards, this happens quickly. But as your deck grows, it takes longer for new purchases to appear. This is why deck thinning matters.

walkthrough.title: A Few Turns Later: First Action Card
walkthrough.text: A few turns have passed and Player 2 draws a hand with their Village in it. Time to use it! During the Action Phase, Player 2 plays the Village. They draw one card and gain two Actions. Now they have two Actions remaining. If they have another Action card in hand, they can play it. If not, they move to the Buy Phase. Either way, that Village just made their turn better.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Forgetting Treasures
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has been buying Action cards and gets excited. They play a Smithy, drawing three cards. Great hand! But then they immediately try to buy a Province without playing their Treasure cards first. Hold on. You must play your Treasures during the Buy Phase to generate coins. Action cards that give plus Coins add to your total, but Treasure cards in your hand do nothing unless you play them. Always remember to play your Treasures before buying.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Too Many Terminals
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has been buying lots of Action cards that looked powerful. They bought a Smithy, a Market, another Smithy, and a Militia. The problem is that Smithy and Militia are both terminal actions. They do not give plus Actions. Player 1 draws a hand with Smithy and Militia together. They can only play one of them. The other sits dead in their hand, completely wasted. This is called a terminal collision.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Terminals with Villages
walkthrough.text: The fix for terminal collisions is simple. For every terminal Action you add to your deck, make sure you also have cards that give plus Actions, like Village or Festival. Think of it as a ratio. If you have two terminals, you need at least one card giving plus two Actions to play them both. Without that support, your expensive Action cards will keep crashing into each other.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Buying Victory Cards Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has six coins on their third turn and gets tempted by Gold, which costs six coins. But then they notice the Province pile, also six coins. A Province is worth six victory points! They buy the Province. Sounds smart, right? Wrong. It is way too early. That Province goes into their tiny deck and will show up in their hand constantly. Victory cards do nothing during the game. They just sit in your hand taking up space where a useful card could be.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Your Engine Before Buying Green
walkthrough.text: This is one of the most important lessons in Dominion. Victory cards, the ones with the green borders, are dead weight during the game. They clog your hand because you cannot play them. Buy them too early and your deck becomes sluggish. The golden rule is to build your buying power first with Silvers, Golds, and Action cards. Then start buying Provinces once you can reliably afford them. Experienced players call this avoiding green bloat.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Trash Cards
walkthrough.text: The Kingdom cards include Chapel, which lets you trash up to four cards from your hand. Player 1 ignores it completely. Meanwhile, Player 2 buys a Chapel on their next turn. Over the next few turns, Player 2 trashes their starting Coppers and Estates. Their deck shrinks from ten cards to six cards, but those six cards are all useful. Player 1 still has all seven Coppers and three Estates dragging their deck down.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Trash Your Starting Cards
walkthrough.text: Your starting Coppers and Estates are the weakest cards in the game. Copper only gives one coin, and Estates just clog your hand with one measly victory point. When a card like Chapel, Remodel, or Mine is available, use it to trash those weak cards. A lean deck of eight great cards will outperform a bloated deck of fifteen mediocre ones every time. Trashing is one of the strongest strategies in Dominion.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Forgetting Where Bought Cards Go
walkthrough.text: Player 1 buys a Laboratory and excitedly tries to put it on top of their draw pile so they can use it next turn. That is not how it works. Bought cards always go to your discard pile. You will not see that Laboratory until your deck reshuffles. This is true for every card you buy or gain unless a card effect specifically says otherwise.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game: Building Toward Provinces
walkthrough.text: Several turns have passed. Player 2 has trashed their weak cards and built a lean deck with Silver, Gold, and a few Action cards. They are now consistently hitting eight coins per turn, which is the magic number for buying Provinces. Player 1 has a bigger deck but draws inconsistent hands because they never trashed anything. See the difference?

walkthrough.title: Watching the Supply Piles
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, keep an eye on how many cards remain in each Supply pile. Remember, the game ends when the Province pile is empty or any three piles are empty. A savvy player can trigger the three-pile ending strategically by emptying cheap piles. If you are ahead in victory points, you might want to end the game quickly. If you are behind, you want to keep it going.

walkthrough.title: The Province Push
walkthrough.text: Player 2 starts buying Provinces. Each one is worth six victory points. Yes, they clog the deck, but at this stage it does not matter because the game is ending soon. Player 2 buys a Province every turn they can afford one. This is the right time to go green, when you have the engine to sustain it and the game is in its final stretch.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The last Province is bought and the game ends. Both players gather all their cards, draw pile, discard pile, and hand. Player 1 counts three Estates at one victory point each and one Province for six points, totaling nine victory points. But they also have two Curses from an opponent's Attack card, so subtract two for a total of seven. Player 2 has trashed their Estates but bought three Provinces and two Duchies, totaling twenty-four victory points. Player 2 wins by a landslide, thanks to a lean, efficient deck.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all three turn phases in action, learned about terminal collisions, green bloat, trashing, and deck cycling. The beautiful thing about Dominion is that every game plays differently because the ten Kingdom cards change. Once you are comfortable with the basics, start experimenting with different card combinations.

walkthrough.title: Before You Shuffle Up
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Shuffle Up
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for card types and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest traps new players fall into. First, buying Victory cards too early and clogging their deck with green cards that do nothing. Second, loading up on terminal Actions without any plus Actions support, leading to constant collisions. Third, ignoring trash cards when they are available, keeping a bloated deck of weak cards. Fourth, forgetting to play Treasures before buying. Fifth, treating every Kingdom card as equally worth buying. A focused deck beats a deck with one of everything. Keep these in mind and you will skip the most painful learning curve.

walkthrough.title: Opening Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your first two turns set the tone for the entire game. On your first hand of five cards, you will have either four coins or three coins depending on whether you drew four Coppers or three. With four coins, Silver is almost always the right buy. With three coins, look for a strong two-cost or three-cost Kingdom card. If a powerful trasher like Chapel is available for two coins, buy it on your three-coin hand. A common strong opening is Silver plus Chapel. This gives you economy and a way to thin your deck immediately.

walkthrough.title: Deck Thinning Is King
walkthrough.text: The single most impactful thing you can do in Dominion is thin your deck. Your starting cards are terrible. Copper gives only one coin, and Estates are dead draws worth just one victory point. Every time you trash one of these, your average hand quality goes up. A deck of eight good cards will draw a powerful hand every single turn. A deck of twenty mixed cards will give you random, inconsistent hands. Whenever trashing cards are available in the Kingdom, prioritize them highly.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Card Types
walkthrough.text: Think of Dominion cards in three categories. Engine cards generate resources or enable other cards. These are your Action cards with plus Cards, plus Actions, and plus Coins. Payload cards convert those resources into victory points. Gold and other high-coin cards are payload because they buy Provinces. Green cards are your victory points but do nothing during the game. Your goal is to build an engine, add payload, and then buy green once your engine is running. If you skip the engine step, your green purchases will slow you down.

walkthrough.title: Money Strategy vs. Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: There are two main approaches to Dominion. Big Money focuses on buying Treasure cards, especially Silver and Gold, and ignoring most Action cards. You buy Provinces whenever you hit eight coins. It is simple and reliable. Engine strategy focuses on chaining Action cards that draw your whole deck and generate huge amounts of coins in one turn. Engines are more powerful but harder to build and require the right Kingdom cards. When in doubt, lean toward Big Money with a couple of strong Action cards added in. As you get more experienced, you will learn when the Kingdom supports a full engine build.

walkthrough.title: The Terminal Action Rule of Thumb
walkthrough.text: Here is a practical rule for managing terminal Actions. Count the terminal Actions in your deck and count the cards that give plus two or more Actions. You want roughly one village-type card for every two terminal Actions. If you have four terminals and only one Village, you will have collision problems. If you find yourself with too many terminals, stop buying them and invest in plus Actions cards or switch to Treasures. A three-terminal deck with two Villages is much stronger than a five-terminal deck with one Village.

walkthrough.title: When to Start Buying Provinces
walkthrough.text: Timing your Province purchases is one of the most important skills in Dominion. Buy Provinces too early and you clog your deck. Buy them too late and your opponent grabs them all. A good rule of thumb: start buying Provinces when you can reliably generate eight coins in most hands. For Big Money strategies, this usually happens around turn ten to twelve. For engine strategies, it might happen later but then you buy multiple per turn. Also watch the Province pile count. If only four Provinces remain, you need to start buying now or you will run out of chances.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to the Kingdom
walkthrough.text: Every game of Dominion is different because the ten Kingdom cards change. Before your first buy, scan all ten cards and ask yourself three questions. Is there a good trasher? If yes, deck thinning should be a priority. Are there strong plus Actions cards? If yes, you can build an engine with multiple terminals. Are there powerful Attacks? If yes, consider buying a Reaction card for defense. The Kingdom dictates your strategy, not the other way around. Do not try to force a strategy that the available cards do not support.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't buy Victory cards too early — they clog your deck (green bloat)
Don't overload on terminal Actions without +Actions support
Don't ignore trashing — thin your deck of Coppers/Estates
Always play Treasures before buying
A focused deck beats one-of-everything

summary.title: Opening Strategy
bullets: 4-coin hand → buy Silver (almost always correct)
3-coin hand → look for strong 2-3 cost Kingdom card
Classic strong opening: Silver + Chapel (or other trasher)
First 2 turns set the tone for the whole game

summary.title: Deck Thinning Is King
bullets: Trash Coppers and Estates whenever possible
8 great cards > 20 mixed cards for hand consistency
Prioritize trashers (Chapel, Remodel, Mine) highly
Every trashed junk card = permanent improvement

summary.title: Understanding Card Types
bullets: Engine cards = +Cards, +Actions, +Coins (enablers)
Payload cards = Gold, high-coin cards (buy Provinces)
Green cards = VP but dead weight during play
Build engine → add payload → buy green

summary.title: Money Strategy vs. Engine Strategy
bullets: Big Money: Silver/Gold focus, buy Province at 8 coins — simple and reliable
Engine: chain Actions to draw deck + generate huge coin turns
When in doubt, lean Big Money + a few strong Actions
Full engine requires the right Kingdom card support

summary.title: The Terminal Action Rule of Thumb
bullets: ~1 Village-type card per 2 terminal Actions
Too many terminals → constant collisions
Stop buying terminals if you lack +Actions support
3 terminals + 2 Villages > 5 terminals + 1 Village

summary.title: When to Start Buying Provinces
bullets: Buy Provinces once you reliably hit 8 coins most turns
Big Money: ~turn 10-12 — Engine: later but buy multiple per turn
Watch Province pile count — 4 left = start buying now
Too early = green bloat — too late = opponent takes them all

summary.title: Pay Attention to the Kingdom
bullets: Scan all 10 Kingdom cards before your first buy
Good trasher available? → prioritize deck thinning
Strong +Actions cards? → engine build is viable
Attacks present? → consider Reaction card defense
Let the Kingdom dictate strategy, not the other way around

walkthrough.title: Big Money with a Splash
walkthrough.text: Pure Big Money, buying only Treasures and Provinces, is surprisingly competitive. But it gets even better when you splash in one or two strong Action cards. The classic version is Big Money plus Smithy. You buy mostly Silver and Gold, add one or two Smithies for card draw, and start buying Provinces around turn ten. The Smithy draws three extra cards, making it much more likely you hit eight coins. This strategy is your baseline. If you cannot figure out a better plan from the Kingdom, Big Money with a draw card is always solid.

walkthrough.title: Village-Smithy Engine
walkthrough.text: The Village-Smithy engine is the most classic Dominion combo and a great introduction to engine building. Village gives plus one Card and plus two Actions. Smithy gives plus three Cards. Together, they let you draw through large chunks of your deck. The idea is to play a Village, then a Smithy, then another Village, then another Smithy, chaining draws until your whole deck is in your hand. Once you see all your Treasure cards at once, you can generate enormous buying power in a single turn. The key is getting the right ratio of Villages to Smithies, roughly one to one.

walkthrough.title: Aggressive Trashing
walkthrough.text: When Chapel or another powerful trasher is in the Kingdom, aggressive trashing is often the best strategy. The goal is to trash all seven starting Coppers and all three starting Estates as quickly as possible. Yes, even the Coppers. Chapel can trash up to four cards per play, so in just two or three uses, your entire starting deck is gone. What remains is a tiny deck of only the good cards you have bought. Hands become incredibly consistent. Pair aggressive trashing with a few key purchases like Silver, a draw card, and an Action card that generates coins.

walkthrough.title: The Three-Pile Rush
walkthrough.text: Most beginners focus only on Provinces, but emptying three Supply piles is an alternative win condition that can catch opponents off guard. If you are buying lots of cheap cards and notice two piles getting low, you can intentionally empty a third pile to end the game while you are ahead. This works especially well with cards that gain other cards, quickly emptying piles. Watch the Supply constantly. If an opponent is building a slow engine that will eventually be powerful, you can rush the game ending before they get going.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Kingdom
walkthrough.text: The most important advanced skill is reading the Kingdom, looking at the ten available cards and identifying the strongest strategy before making your first buy. Ask yourself these questions. Is there a trasher? If yes, lean toward engine play. Are there strong villages and draw cards? If yes, a full engine is viable. Are there powerful Attacks with no Reaction available? If yes, lean toward simpler strategies that are less disrupted by attacks. Is there a strong two-card combo? Many Kingdom cards synergize in pairs. Reading the Kingdom is what separates intermediate players from advanced players.

walkthrough.title: Counting and Deck Tracking
walkthrough.text: Advanced players keep a mental count of the key cards in their deck and discard pile. If you know you have two Golds and one Silver left in your draw pile with only five cards remaining, you can predict that your next hand will have at least six coins. This lets you make better decisions about when to buy Provinces versus when to keep building. You are allowed to look through your own discard pile at any time. Start by just counting your Golds and Victory cards. Over time, this becomes second nature.

walkthrough.title: Duchy Dancing
walkthrough.text: When the Province pile gets low, usually three or four remaining, the game enters a critical end phase. Duchies become much more important because the game might end before you can buy another Province. A Duchy costs five coins and is worth three victory points. If you cannot hit eight coins, buying a Duchy is often better than buying Gold at six coins. Advanced players also watch for the point spread. If you are ahead by five points and there are only two Provinces left, you can start buying Duchies to maintain your lead while making it impossible for your opponent to catch up.

walkthrough.title: Action Card Synergies
walkthrough.text: The real depth of Dominion comes from discovering how Kingdom cards combine. A Throne Room, which lets you play an Action card twice, is mediocre on its own. But pair it with a card that gives plus three Coins and suddenly you have plus six Coins from two cards. Look for combos where one card amplifies another. Trash-for-benefit cards like Remodel become incredible when you can trash a Gold to gain a Province. Cards that gain other cards become engines of their own. Every set of ten Kingdom cards has hidden synergies waiting to be found.

summary.title: Big Money with a Splash
bullets: Buy Silver/Gold + 1-2 draw cards (e.g., Smithy)
Province buying starts ~turn 10
Simple, reliable baseline strategy
Use when Kingdom doesn't support a full engine

summary.title: Village-Smithy Engine
bullets: Village (+1 Card, +2 Actions) + Smithy (+3 Cards) = draw chains
Goal: draw entire deck in one turn for massive buying power
Ratio: ~1:1 Villages to Smithies
Classic engine — great intro to combo play

summary.title: Aggressive Trashing
bullets: Trash ALL 7 Coppers and 3 Estates as fast as possible
Chapel can trash 4 per play → done in 2-3 uses
Tiny deck = incredibly consistent hands
Pair with Silver + draw card + coin-generating Action

summary.title: The Three-Pile Rush
bullets: Alternative win: empty any 3 Supply piles to end the game
Catches engine-builders off guard before they get going
Works well with cards that gain other cards cheaply
Watch Supply counts constantly — 2 low piles = rush opportunity

summary.title: Reading the Kingdom
bullets: Identify the strongest strategy before your first buy
Trasher available? → lean engine — No trasher? → lean Big Money
Strong villages + draw? → full engine viable
Look for 2-card combos that synergize powerfully

summary.title: Counting and Deck Tracking
bullets: Track key cards remaining in draw pile for better decisions
You may look through your own discard pile at any time
Start simple: count Golds and Victory cards
Predict coin totals → decide Province vs. continue building

summary.title: Duchy Dancing
bullets: When 3-4 Provinces remain, Duchies (3 VP, cost 5) become critical
Can't hit 8 coins? Duchy > Gold when game is ending
Ahead on points? Buy Duchies to lock your lead
Watch point spread — calculate if opponent can catch up

summary.title: Action Card Synergies
bullets: Throne Room + high-value Action = doubled effect
Trash-for-benefit: Remodel Gold → gain Province
Gainers become engines — free cards = fast pile depletion
Every Kingdom has hidden combos — finding them is the skill

entries.definition: The first phase of each turn. You may play one Action card from your hand. Additional Actions can be played if you have gained +Actions from cards played earlier this phase.
The second phase of each turn. Play Treasure cards from your hand to generate coins, then spend coins to buy cards from the Supply. You get one Buy per turn unless increased by card effects.
The third and final phase of each turn. All cards in play and remaining in hand go to your discard pile. Then draw five new cards from your draw pile for your next turn.
The weakest Treasure card, worth one coin when played. Every player starts with seven Coppers. Costs zero to buy. Often a target for trashing to thin your deck.
A mid-tier Treasure card worth two coins when played. Costs three to buy. A reliable early purchase that improves hand quality.
The strongest basic Treasure card, worth three coins when played. Costs six to buy. The primary payload card for Big Money strategies.
The weakest Victory card, worth one victory point at game end. Every player starts with three Estates. Costs two to buy. A common trashing target because it provides minimal VP and clogs your hand.
A mid-tier Victory card worth three victory points at game end. Costs five to buy. Becomes important in the endgame when Provinces are running low.
The most valuable basic Victory card, worth six victory points at game end. Costs eight to buy. The primary goal of most strategies. When the Province pile empties, the game ends immediately.

A card worth negative one victory point at game end. Curses are not in any player's starting deck but are given to opponents by certain Attack cards like Witch. Costs zero to buy but nobody would want to.
A card type played during the Buy Phase to generate coins. The three basic Treasures are Copper (1 coin), Silver (2 coins), and Gold (3 coins). Some Kingdom cards are also Treasures.

A card type that provides victory points at the end of the game but does nothing during play. Basic Victory cards are Estate (1 VP), Duchy (3 VP), and Province (6 VP). Some Kingdom cards are also Victory cards.

A card type played during the Action Phase. Action cards have various effects such as drawing cards, providing extra Actions, generating coins, or attacking opponents. Kingdom card piles are mostly Action cards.

A subtype of Action card that negatively affects other players when played. Effects include forcing opponents to discard, gain Curses, or reveal and modify their hands. Blocked by Reaction cards like Moat.
A subtype of Action card that can be revealed from your hand in response to an Attack to negate or modify its effect. The most common Reaction is Moat, which completely blocks an Attack.
An Action card effect that lets you draw additional cards from your draw pile into your hand. For example, +3 Cards means draw three cards. This is the primary way to see more of your deck each turn.

An Action card effect that grants additional Action plays during your Action Phase. You start each turn with one Action. +2 Actions means you can play two more Action cards after the current one resolves.

An Action card effect that adds virtual coins to your total for the Buy Phase. These coins combine with coins from Treasure cards. For example, +3 Coins plus two Coppers gives you five coins total to spend.

An Action card effect that grants additional purchases during your Buy Phase. You start each turn with one Buy. +1 Buy means you can buy two cards this turn instead of one, spending from your total coin pool.

The shared collection of card piles available for purchase. Includes Copper, Silver, Gold, Estate, Duchy, Province, Curse, and the ten chosen Kingdom card piles. Cards are always bought from the Supply.

The ten types of cards chosen for each game from the twenty-five available in the box. Each pile has ten copies. Kingdom cards are mostly Action cards but can include Treasure, Victory, or multi-type cards. They define the unique strategy of each game.
Your personal face-down deck of cards. You draw from the top of this pile to fill your hand. When it runs out, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw pile.
Your personal face-up pile of used cards. Cards go here during Clean-up and when you buy or gain new cards. When your draw pile is empty, your discard pile is shuffled to become your new draw pile.

A shared pile where permanently removed cards are placed. Trashed cards are out of the game entirely and cannot be recovered unless a specific card effect allows it. Trashing weak cards is a key strategy for improving deck quality.

To acquire a card and add it to your discard pile without spending coins or using a Buy. Cards are gained through Action card effects. Gaining is distinct from buying, though both typically send cards to your discard pile.

To spend coins during your Buy Phase to acquire a card from the Supply. The purchased card goes to your discard pile. You get one Buy per turn unless increased by +Buy effects. Buying uses a Buy and coins; gaining does not.

To show a card to all players. Some card effects ask you to reveal cards from your hand or the top of your deck. Revealed cards are shown briefly, then returned to where they came from unless the effect says otherwise.

To place a card temporarily out of play, usually face up next to your mat. Set-aside cards are returned to a specific location as instructed by the card effect. They are not in your hand, deck, or discard while set aside.

An Action card that does not provide +Actions. Playing a terminal ends your Action Phase unless you have leftover Actions from a previously played card. Too many terminals cause collisions where one sits unplayable in your hand.
When you draw two or more terminal Action cards in the same hand and can only play one of them. The unplayed terminals are wasted. Avoided by balancing terminals with +Actions cards like Village.

When a player buys too many Victory cards too early, filling their deck with green cards that cannot be played. This makes hands weak and inconsistent because Victory cards take up space where useful cards could be drawn.

The strategy of removing weak cards like Copper and Estate from your deck via trashing. A thinner deck cycles faster and draws more consistent, powerful hands. One of the strongest fundamental strategies in Dominion.

A deck built around chaining Action cards to draw many cards, generate large amounts of coins, and potentially buy multiple cards per turn. Engines require +Actions cards, draw cards, and usually trashing to work effectively.

A strategy focused on buying Treasure cards (Silver, Gold) with minimal Action card purchases, then buying Provinces whenever eight coins are reached. Simple and reliable, often used as a baseline to compare other strategies against.
When your draw pile is empty and you need to draw cards, shuffle your entire discard pile to create a new face-down draw pile. This is how cards you buy eventually appear in your hand.
Kingdom Action card costing two. Gives +1 Action, then discard any number of cards from your hand, and draw that many cards. Useful for cycling past bad cards to find better ones.

Kingdom Action card costing two. Trash up to four cards from your hand. One of the most powerful cards in the game despite its low cost. Enables aggressive deck thinning of starting Coppers and Estates.
Kingdom Action-Reaction card costing two. Gives +2 Cards. As a Reaction, reveal from hand when another player plays an Attack to be unaffected by it. The primary defense against Attack cards.
Kingdom Action card costing three. Gives +1 Card and +1 Action, then look through your discard pile and optionally put a card from it on top of your draw pile. Useful for recycling a key card.
Kingdom Action card costing three. Gives +1 Card and +1 Action. The first time you play a Silver this turn, +1 Coin. Rewards Silver-heavy strategies.
Kingdom Action card costing three. Gives +1 Card and +2 Actions. The classic enabler for playing multiple Action cards per turn. Essential for engine strategies to avoid terminal collisions.
Kingdom Action card costing three. Gain a card costing up to four from the Supply. Provides a free card each time it is played, useful for building your deck quickly without spending coins.

Kingdom Action-Attack card costing four. Gain a Silver onto the top of your deck. Each other player reveals a Victory card from their hand and puts it on top of their deck, or reveals a hand with no Victory cards.
Kingdom Victory card costing four. Worth one victory point per ten cards in your entire deck, rounded down. Rewards strategies that acquire many cards rather than thinning.
Kingdom Action-Attack card costing four. Gives +2 Coins. Each other player discards down to three cards in hand. A strong Attack that shrinks opponents' options.
Kingdom Action card costing four. You may trash a Copper from your hand for +3 Coins. Combines trashing with a big coin boost, especially powerful in the early game.
Kingdom Action card costing four. Gives +1 Card, +1 Action, and +1 Coin. Then discard one card per empty Supply pile. A solid all-around card that gets slightly worse as piles empty.
Kingdom Action card costing four. Trash a card from your hand and gain a card costing up to two more than the trashed card. Enables upgrading weak cards into stronger ones.
Kingdom Action card costing four. Gives +3 Cards. A powerful terminal draw card. The backbone of many Big Money and engine strategies. Draws three cards but provides no +Actions.
Kingdom Action card costing four. You may play an Action card from your hand twice. Doubles the effect of any Action card, making it incredibly versatile. Effectively a multiplier for your best card.
Kingdom Action-Attack card costing five. Gain a Gold. Each other player reveals the top two cards of their deck, trashes a revealed Treasure other than Copper, and discards the rest.
Kingdom Action card costing five. Gives +4 Cards and +1 Buy. Each other player draws a card. Massive draw power with the downside of helping opponents too.
Kingdom Action card costing five. Gives +2 Actions, +1 Buy, and +1 Coin. A strong village variant that also provides economy and extra buying power.
Kingdom Action card costing five. Gives +2 Cards and +1 Action. A non-terminal draw card, meaning you can play it and still play another Action. One of the strongest cards in the base game.
Kingdom Action card costing five. Draw until you have seven cards in hand. You may set aside Action cards drawn this way and discard them afterward. Great for filling your hand past the normal five.
Kingdom Action card costing five. Gives +1 Card, +1 Action, +1 Buy, and +1 Coin. Does a little of everything. A solid, versatile card that never hurts to have in your deck.
Kingdom Action card costing five. You may trash a Treasure from your hand to gain a Treasure costing up to three more, putting it into your hand. Upgrades Copper to Silver or Silver to Gold.

Kingdom Action card costing five. Gives +1 Card and +1 Action. Look at the top two cards of your deck. Trash and/or discard any of them, then put the rest back in any order. Powerful for deck management.
Kingdom Action-Attack card costing five. Gives +2 Cards. Each other player gains a Curse. One of the strongest Attacks because Curses are worth negative one victory point and clog opponents' decks.
Kingdom Action card costing six. Gain a card costing up to five to your hand. Put a card from your hand onto your deck. Provides a free high-value card and lets you set up your next draw.
